title: Sensor Connectivity and Heartbeat Monitoring (Meraki MT)
dataSources: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input plus `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:devicesavailabilities` from `cisco_meraki_devices_availabilities` in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Used to detect sensors that have stopped reporting.
spl:
index=meraki sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory"
| stats latest(_time) as last_reading by serial, "network.name"
| eval hours_since_reading=round((now()-last_reading)/3600, 1)
| where hours_since_reading > 2
| eval last_reading_human=strftime(last_reading, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| sort - hours_since_reading
description: Ensures all sensors maintain connectivity and operational status.
value: Ensures all sensors maintain connectivity and operational status.
implementation: Track when each MT sensor last reported. Sensors transmit periodically (every few minutes for live metrics, hourly for slow-change ones); >2 hours without any reading typically means the sensor has disconnected, lost LoRa coverage, or the gateway (MR/MV) is offline. Cross-reference with `meraki:devicesavailabilities` for the parent gateway state.
visualization: Sensor status table; last heartbeat timeline; offline sensor list.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable (no CIM or Operational_Telemetry data model in this SPL).
knownFalsePositives: Benign events during building tests, after-hours schedules, and vendor work—confirm against the BMS console before urgent response.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input plus `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:devicesavailabilities` from `cisco_meraki_devices_availabilities` in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Used to detect sensors that have stopped reporting..
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Track when each MT sensor last reported. Sensors transmit periodically (every few minutes for live metrics, hourly for slow-change ones); >2 hours without any reading typically means the sensor has disconnected, lost LoRa coverage, or the gateway (MR/MV) is offline. Cross-reference with `meraki:devicesavailabilities` for the parent gateway state.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=meraki sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory"
| stats latest(_time) as last_reading by serial, "network.name"
| eval hours_since_reading=round((now()-last_reading)/3600, 1)
| where hours_since_reading > 2
| eval last_reading_human=strftime(last_reading, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| sort - hours_since_reading
```

Understanding this SPL

**Sensor Connectivity and Heartbeat Monitoring (Meraki MT)** — Ensures all sensors maintain connectivity and operational status.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input plus `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:devicesavailabilities` from `cisco_meraki_devices_availabilities` in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Used to detect sensors that have stopped reporting. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: meraki; **sourcetype**: meraki:sensorreadingshistory. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=meraki, sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by serial, "network.name"** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **hours_since_reading** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where hours_since_reading > 2` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **last_reading_human** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Sensor status table; last heartbeat timeline; offline sensor list.
grandmaExplanation: We watch how your building systems behave. We help you know if something is off with this area: sensor connectivity and heartbeat monitoring before harm, damage, or wasted effort piles up.
